title: The Hilbert Series of the Superspace Coinvariant Ring
overall_summary: The paper proves the doubly graded Hilbert series of the type A superspace coinvariant ring SR_n = Ω_n/SI_n, where Ω_n = C[x_1,...,x_n] ⊗ ∧{θ_1,...,θ_n} and SI_n is generated by S_n-invariants of positive degree. The main formula is Hilb(SR_n;q,z) = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k), where z tracks fermionic degree and Stir_q is the q-Stirling number of the second kind. The core method is indirect: eliminate fermionic variables via a Gale-order triangular straightening, reduce to regular sequences and colon ideals in C[x_1,...,x_n], then prove a matching lower bound using specially constructed differential operators D_J and harmonic theory. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the crucial extract is that the q,z-coefficients of this one-boson/one-fermion superspace Hilbert series have an explicit subset-staircase interpretation; via the standard hook-detection relation between superspace and universal Schur coefficients, this is exactly the kind of formula needed for hook shapes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1, Sagan-Swanson staircase identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a subset J ⊆ [n], define its J-staircase st(J) = (st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) by st(J)_1 = 0 if 1 ∈ J and st(J)_1 = 1 if 1 ∉ J, and recursively st(J)_{i+1} = st(J)_i if i+1 ∈ J while st(J)_{i+1} = st(J)_i + 1 if i+1 ∉ J. Equivalently st(J)_i = i - |J ∩ [i]|. With [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r-1}, [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q, and q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) defined by Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1) with Stir_q(0,0)=1 and Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k ≠ 0, one has the polynomial identity ∑_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this identity from Sagan-Swanson. The identity is compatible with the recurrence for q-Stirling numbers by conditioning on whether n lies in J. For fixed b=|J| and k=n-b, st(J)_n=k. Splitting subsets J⊆[n] according to n∈J gives the same recurrence as A(n,k)=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k): the final factor [k]_q is contributed by st(J)_n, and the remaining product is the corresponding staircase product for n-1 with either k or k-1. Initial cases match. This identity is later used to convert a sum over subsets J of products ∏[st(J)_i]_q into the closed q-Stirling Hilbert series.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2, regular sequence criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f_1,...,f_n be homogeneous polynomials of positive degree in C[x_1,...,x_n]. Then f_1,...,f_n is a regular sequence in C[x_1,...,x_n] if and only if the affine variety V(f_1,...,f_n) ⊆ C^n cut out by these polynomials is exactly the origin {0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard complete-intersection criterion in a polynomial ring over a field: n homogeneous equations in n variables form a regular sequence exactly when their common zero locus has dimension zero. Since the equations are homogeneous, any zero-dimensional cone is supported at the origin. The paper uses this criterion repeatedly to prove that explicitly constructed homogeneous polynomial sequences are regular by proving their only common zero is 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3, colon ideals from Poincare duality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a,a′ ⊆ C[x_1,...,x_n] be homogeneous ideals and let f ∈ C[x_1,...,x_n] be homogeneous of degree k with f ∉ a. Suppose a′ ⊆ (a:f), where (a:f) = {g : gf ∈ a}. If C[x]/a′ is a Poincare duality algebra of socle degree r and C[x]/a is a Poincare duality algebra of socle degree r+k, then a′ = (a:f).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result from Abe-Horiguchi-Masuda-Murai-Sato and uses it as a black box. The conceptual mechanism is that in a Poincare duality algebra, annihilators are controlled by perfect multiplication pairings. The inclusion a′⊆(a:f) gives a quotient map C[x]/a′ → C[x]/(a:f). The socle-degree shift by deg f and the nonvanishing of f modulo a force the annihilator of multiplication by f to be exactly a′, leaving no room for a larger colon ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1, differential stability of the supercoinvariant ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Ω_n = C[x_1,...,x_n] ⊗ ∧{θ_1,...,θ_n}, with S_n acting diagonally on x_i and θ_i. Let SI_n be the ideal generated by S_n-invariant superspace elements with zero constant term, and let I_n ⊆ C[x_1,...,x_n] be the classical coinvariant ideal generated by positive-degree symmetric polynomials. Let d:Ω_n→Ω_n be the Euler differential d(f)=∑_{i=1}^n ∂f/∂x_i · θ_i. Then SI_n contains I_n and is closed under d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Containment I_n⊆SI_n is immediate because symmetric polynomials in the x-variables are S_n-invariant elements of Ω_n. The operator d commutes with the diagonal S_n-action, satisfies d∘d=0, and obeys the super-Leibniz rule d(fg)=df·g ± f·dg for bihomogeneous f,g. Hence applying d to a generator from the invariant subalgebra gives another invariant-derived element, and applying d to any ideal multiple remains in the same ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.2 and equation (3.8), construction of triangular supercoinvariant elements q_{J,i}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], write θ_J for the increasing product of θ_j with j∈J, and order subsets of the same size by Gale order: {a_1<...<a_r} ≤_Gale {b_1<...<b_r} if a_i≤b_i for all i. Let h_r(S) be the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial of degree r in variables {x_s : s∈S}. For each J⊆[n] and 1≤i≤n, define q_{J,i}∈Ω_n as follows, with the convention min(∅)=∞. If i<min(J), set q_{J,i}=h_i({i,i+1,...,n}) θ_J. If i≥min(J), set s=max(J∩[i]), set r=n-|J∪{i+1,...,n}|+1, and set q_{J,i}=d h_r(J∪{i+1,...,n}) · θ_{J\{s}}. Define p_{J,i}∈C[x_1,...,x_n] by p_{J,i}=h_i({i,...,n}) in the first case, and p_{J,i}=∂_s h_r(J∪{i+1,...,n}) in the second case. Then q_{J,i}∈SI_n, q_{J,i} is bihomogeneous of bosonic degree st(J)_i and fermionic degree |J|, and q_{J,i} lies in ⊕_{J≤_Gale K} C[x]θ_K. More precisely, q_{J,i} ≡ p_{J,i} θ_J modulo ⊕_{J<_Gale K} C[x]θ_K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The membership q_{J,i}∈SI_n uses two facts: h_r(S)∈I_n whenever r>n-|S|, and SI_n is closed under d by Lemma 3.1. The degree statement follows directly from the pointer construction: moving from i to i+1 increases the relevant complete-homogeneous degree exactly when the next position is not in J, reproducing the staircase st(J). For the Gale triangularity, expand d h_r(S)=∑_{k∈S} ∂_k h_r(S) θ_k and then multiply by θ_{J\{s}}. The surviving fermionic monomials replace the removed index s by an index k weakly to the right in the sense forced by the construction, so their indexing sets K satisfy J≤_Gale K; the coefficient of the unchanged set J is exactly p_{J,i}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3, complete-homogeneous derivative identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S⊆[n], let a,b∈S, let c∉S, and let r>1. Then the following identity holds in C[x_1,...,x_n]: ∂_a h_r(S) = ∂_b h_r(S) + (x_c-x_b) ∂_b h_{r-1}(S∪{c}) - (x_c-x_a) ∂_a h_{r-1}(S∪{c}). Here h_d(T) is the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial of degree d in the variables indexed by T.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof expands the right-hand side and groups terms using h_r(S∪{c}) = h_r(S) + x_c h_{r-1}(S∪{c}). After rewriting, the expression becomes ∂_a h_r(S) plus two correction terms of the form ∂_b(h_r(S∪{c})-x_b h_{r-1}(S∪{c})) and ∂_a(h_r(S∪{c})-x_a h_{r-1}(S∪{c})) with opposite signs. The first correction is zero because h_r(S∪{c})-x_b h_{r-1}(S∪{c}) = h_r((S∪{c})\{b}) is independent of x_b; the second vanishes similarly. This identity is the technical engine that moves partial derivatives from one variable to another inside the ideals generated by the p_{J,i}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4, strategic partial derivatives lie in the p_{J,i}-ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], let I_J=(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n})⊆C[x_1,...,x_n], where p_{J,i} are the polynomials defined from J as in Lemmas 3.2 and equation (3.8). Then for every j∈J, one has ∂_j h_{n-|J|+1}(J) ∈ I_J, where h_{n-|J|+1}(J) is the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial in variables {x_j : j∈J}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof establishes a stronger claim using only the tail ideal I′_J=(p_{J,j_0},p_{J,j_0+1},...,p_{J,n}) in C[x_{j_0},...,x_n], where j_0=min(J). It inducts on the number of variables in this tail. If J is a terminal interval, the desired derivatives are already among the generators. Otherwise choose c_0 to be the smallest integer greater than j_0 that is not in J, and let S={j_0,j_0+1,...,n}\{c_0}. Repeated applications of Lemma 3.3 transfer membership of derivatives step-by-step leftward across the gap c_0. This shows that a modified generating set not involving x_{c_0} lies in I′_J. After deleting x_{c_0}, the situation identifies with the same problem for the cyclically shifted subset J′=(J\{j_0})∪{c_0} in a smaller variable set, so induction completes the proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5, regular sequence p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J⊆[n] and let st(J)=(st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n). If 1∉J, then the polynomials p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} form a regular sequence in C[x_1,...,x_n], with deg p_{J,i}=st(J)_i for every i. If 1∈J, then p_{J,1}=1, so the ideal they generate is the unit ideal and the quotient is zero. Consequently, in all cases the quotient C[x]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}) has Hilbert series ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q, interpreting [0]_q=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The degree assertion is inherited from Lemma 3.2. For 1∉J, Lemma 2.2 reduces regularity to proving that the common zero locus of the p_{J,i} is {0}. Lemma 3.4 implies that any common zero also zeros all polynomials ∂_j h_{n-|J|+1}(J) for j∈J. A result of Swanson-Wallach says these partial derivatives have only the origin as a common zero in the coordinate subspace spanned by variables indexed by J, so all coordinates x_j with j∈J vanish. After setting these variables to zero, the remaining p_{J,i} for i∉J become triangular homogeneous polynomials in the remaining variables, forcing those coordinates to vanish as well. Since the sequence is homogeneous and regular, the Hilbert series of the complete intersection is the product of q-integers corresponding to the degrees.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6 and Proposition 3.7, abstract straightening and Hilbert-series upper bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every J⊆[n], choose a homogeneous set B_n(J)⊆C[x_1,...,x_n] whose image is a vector-space basis of C[x]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); by Lemma 3.5 its degree generating function is ∑_{m∈B_n(J)} q^{deg m} = ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q. Then for every f∈C[x] there is an expression f θ_J = ∑_{m∈B_n(J)} c_{f,m} m θ_J + g + Σ, where c_{f,m}∈C, g∈SI_n, and Σ∈⊕_{J<_Gale K} C[x]θ_K. Consequently the images in SR_n=Ω_n/SI_n of the union ⨆_{J⊆[n]} B_n(J)θ_J span SR_n, and Hilb(SR_n;q,z) ≤ ∑_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because B_n(J) is a basis modulo the ideal generated by p_{J,i}, any f∈C[x] can be written as f=∑ c_{f,m}m + ∑_i A_i p_{J,i}. Multiplying by θ_J and using q_{J,i}≡p_{J,i}θ_J modulo higher Gale terms replaces each A_i p_{J,i}θ_J by A_i q_{J,i} plus a higher-Gale error. Since every q_{J,i} lies in SI_n, this gives the stated straightening relation. A descending induction on Gale order shows that the union of all B_n(J)θ_J spans the quotient SR_n. Taking degree generating functions gives the upper bound, and Lemma 2.1 converts the subset sum into the q-Stirling expression.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.7, membership criterion for f_J in the classical coinvariant ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], define f_J = ∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i) ∈ C[x_1,...,x_n]. Let I_n be the classical coinvariant ideal. Then f_J∈I_n if and only if 1∈J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If 1∈J, it suffices to show f_{ {1} }=x_1(x_1-x_2)...(x_1-x_n) lies in I_n. Modulo I_n, all positive-degree symmetric polynomials vanish, so the generating-function identity for elementary symmetric functions gives (−1)^d e_d(x_2,...,x_n) ≡ x_1^d modulo I_n. Expanding f_{ {1} } in terms of e_d(x_2,...,x_n) yields f_{ {1} }≡n x_1^n≡0, since x_1^n∈I_n. Conversely, if 1∉J, it is enough to consider the largest such subset J_0={2,3,...,n}, because f_J divides f_{J_0}. The polynomial f_{J_0}=x_2...x_n∏_{2≤r<s≤n}(x_r-x_s) is, up to sign, the sum of the terms of the Vandermonde δ_n in which x_1 does not appear. Thus f_{J_0}⊙δ_n is a nonzero scalar multiple of f_{J_0}⊙f_{J_0}, hence nonzero under the harmonic pairing. Since ann(δ_n)=I_n, f_{J_0}∉I_n, and therefore no divisor f_J with 1∉J lies in I_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.2, 4.5, and 4.8, triangular differential operators D_J
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let d_r:Ω_n→Ω_n be the higher Euler operator d_r(g)=∑_{i=1}^n ∂_i^r(g) θ_i, and for I={i_1<...<i_s} write d_I=d_{i_1}...d_{i_s}. Let H be the n by n lower-triangular matrix H_{a,b}=h_{a-b}(x_a,x_{a+1},...,x_n), with h_0=1 and h_t=0 for t<0. For K⊆[n], set K^*={n-k+1:k∈K}. For J⊆[n], define D_J = ∑_{|I|=|J|} (−1)^{∑_{i∈I} i} Δ_{[n]\J,([n]\I)^*}(H) ⊙ d_I, where Δ denotes the indicated minor and ⊙ means applying a polynomial as a differential operator. Then for every f∈C[x_1,...,x_n], D_J(f)=∑_{|K|=|J|} (F_{J,K}⊙f) θ_K for certain polynomials F_{J,K}. These polynomials satisfy F_{J,K}=0 unless K≤_Gale J, and F_{J,J}=± f_J, where f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j∏_{i>j}(x_j-x_i). Thus D_J(δ_n) has θ_J-coefficient ±(f_J⊙δ_n) and only Gale-lower fermionic terms besides it.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 4.2 computes the θ_K coefficient of d_I(f) as the determinant det(∂_{k_p}^{i_q} f) for K={k_1<...<k_s}. This converts the coefficient of θ_K in D_J(f) into F_{J,K}⊙f, where F_{J,K} is a signed sum of products of minors of H and monomial determinants. Lemma 4.5 packages this signed sum as a single n by n determinant A_{J,K}, whose top block is built from powers x_k^n,...,x_k and whose bottom block consists of the relevant h-polynomial rows from H. Lemma 4.8 evaluates this determinant by factoring ∏_{k∈K}x_k from the top rows and performing column operations from right to left to eliminate complete homogeneous polynomials in the lower block. The result is F_{J,K}=±(∏_{k∈K}x_k) det(∏_{i>j_q}(x_{k_p}-x_i))_{p,q}. If K is not ≤_Gale J this determinant has a southwest zero block crossing the diagonal and vanishes; for K=J it is triangular and its diagonal product is exactly f_J, up to sign.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.10, inclusion p_{J,i}∈(I_n:f_J)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every J⊆[n] and every 1≤i≤n, the polynomial p_{J,i} belongs to the colon ideal (I_n:f_J) = {g∈C[x] : g f_J∈I_n}, where I_n is the classical coinvariant ideal and f_J=∏_{j∈J}x_j∏_{i>j}(x_j-x_i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the triangular supercoinvariant element q_{J,i}=p_{J,i}θ_J plus Gale-higher terms from Lemma 3.2, and apply it to D_J(δ_n), whose θ_J coefficient is ±f_J⊙δ_n and whose other terms are Gale-lower by Lemma 4.8. Swanson-Wallach had shown that the space generated from δ_n by partial derivatives and higher Euler operators lies inside the superharmonic space SH_n, so D_J(δ_n)∈SH_n. Since q_{J,i}∈SI_n and SI_n annihilates SH_n under the ⊙ pairing, q_{J,i}⊙D_J(δ_n)=0. Gale triangularity leaves the θ_J pairing as the only possible contribution, giving (p_{J,i} f_J)⊙δ_n=0. Because ann_{C[x]}(δ_n)=I_n, this is equivalent to p_{J,i} f_J∈I_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11, enumeration of J-Nietzschean permutations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For 1≤j≤n, a permutation w∈S_n is called j-resentful if either w(j)=n or the value w(j)+1 appears among w(j+1),...,w(n). It is j-Nietzschean if it is not j-resentful. For J⊆[n], let N_J be the set of permutations that are j-Nietzschean for every j∈J. Then |N_J| = ∏_{i=1}^n st(J)_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Build a permutation of size n by starting from the unique permutation of size 1 and repeatedly appending a new last letter, standardizing at each step. Suppose a permutation of length k-1 has been built and one wants to append a letter from {1,...,k}. To ensure the final permutation is J-Nietzschean, one forbids appending k if k∈J, and for each previous Nietzschean position j∈J with j<k one forbids appending the value currently equal to v(j)+1, since that would eventually put w(j)+1 to the right of position j. Thus the number of allowed choices at stage k is k-|J∩[k]|=st(J)_k. Multiplying over k gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.12, three realizations of the same ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], let f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j∏_{i>j}(x_j-x_i). Let p_{J,i} be the complete-homogeneous derivative polynomials defined by p_{J,i}=h_i({i,...,n}) if i<min(J), and otherwise p_{J,i}=∂_s h_r(J∪{i+1,...,n}) with s=max(J∩[i]) and r=n-|J∪{i+1,...,n}|+1. Let N_J⊆S_n be the set of J-Nietzschean permutations, embedded in C^n as the coordinate permutations of (1,2,...,n). For a finite set Z⊆C^n, let I(Z) be its vanishing ideal and gr I(Z) the ideal generated by top homogeneous parts of nonzero elements of I(Z). Then the following homogeneous ideals in C[x_1,...,x_n] are equal: (1) the colon ideal (I_n:f_J), (2) the ideal (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}), and (3) gr I(N_J). If I_J denotes this common ideal, then Hilb(C[x]/I_J;q)=∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If 1∈J, then f_J∈I_n by Lemma 4.7, p_{J,1}=1, and N_J is empty, so all three ideals are the unit ideal. Assume 1∉J. Lemma 4.10 gives (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n})⊆(I_n:f_J). Lemma 3.5 makes the p-ideal a complete intersection, hence a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree ∑ st(J)_i − n. The classical coinvariant ring C[x]/I_n is also Poincare duality with socle degree binomial(n,2). The degree of f_J is ∑_{i=1}^n (i−st(J)_i), and these degrees add correctly to binomial(n,2); moreover f_J∉I_n by Lemma 4.7. Lemma 2.3 therefore forces (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n})=(I_n:f_J). For the orbit-harmonic realization, introduce the deformed coinvariant ideal ~I_n=(e_d(x_1,...,x_n)−e_d(1,...,n):1≤d≤n), whose variety is the embedded copy of S_n, and the inhomogeneous polynomial ~f_J=∏_{j∈J}(x_j−n)∏_{i>j}(x_j−x_i+1), whose zero set inside S_n is the J-resentful complement. Thus N_J=V(~I_n)−V(~f_J), and the Nullstellensatz gives I(N_J)=(~I_n:~f_J). Passing to associated graded gives gr I(N_J)⊆(gr ~I_n : f_J)=(I_n:f_J). The quotient by gr I(N_J) has dimension |N_J|, while the quotient by (I_n:f_J) has dimension ∏ st(J)_i by the complete-intersection Hilbert series, and these are equal by Proposition 4.11. Hence the inclusion is equality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1, operator theorem for superharmonics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let SH_n=SI_n^⊥⊆Ω_n be the superharmonic space under the standard superspace differential pairing. Let δ_n=∏_{1≤i<j≤n}(x_j-x_i) be the Vandermonde determinant. For r≥1 let d_r=∑_{i=1}^n ∂_i^r θ_i be the higher Euler operator, and for I={i_1<...<i_s}⊆[n−1] write d_I=d_{i_1}...d_{i_s}. Then SH_n = ∑_{I⊆[n−1]} C[x_1,...,x_n] ⊙ d_I(δ_n). Equivalently, SH_n is the smallest linear subspace of Ω_n containing δ_n and closed under all x-variable partial derivatives ∂_1,...,∂_n and all higher Euler operators d_r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let SH′_n be the right-hand side. Swanson-Wallach had proved SH′_n⊆SH_n. To prove equality, use the operators D_J. For each J, D_J(δ_n)∈SH′_n and has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n with only Gale-lower fermionic terms. By Theorem 4.12, choose a homogeneous basis B_n(J) of C[x]/(I_n:f_J), so the set {g⊙(f_J⊙δ_n):g∈B_n(J)} is linearly independent. Suppose a linear combination of elements g θ_J annihilates SH′_n; by fermionic degree homogeneity and choosing a Gale-minimal J_0 with nonzero coefficient, applying the combination to D_{J_0}(δ_n) isolates exactly the θ_{J_0} leading coefficient and contradicts that linear independence. Therefore dim SH′_n is at least ∑_J |B_n(J)|. Proposition 3.7 gives dim SH_n=dim SR_n at most the same sum, and SH′_n⊆SH_n, so equality holds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2, linear independence criterion in SR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose that for every J⊆[n] one has a homogeneous set C_n(J)⊆C[x_1,...,x_n] whose image in C[x]/(I_n:f_J) is linearly independent. Then the set C_n=⨆_{J⊆[n]} C_n(J) θ_J has linearly independent image in SR_n=Ω_n/SI_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume a nontrivial linear relation among the images of C_n(J)θ_J in SR_n. Equivalently, the corresponding superspace element annihilates SH_n under the harmonic pairing. Choose J_0 Gale-minimal among subsets appearing with nonzero coefficient. Apply the relation as a differential operator to D_{J_0}(δ_n)∈SH_n. The Gale triangularity of D_{J_0}(δ_n) ensures that all terms except the θ_{J_0} component either vanish by fermionic mismatch or involve subsets excluded by the minimality choice. The remaining equality is ∑_{g∈C_n(J_0)} c_g g⊙(f_{J_0}⊙δ_n)=0. Since ann(f_{J_0}⊙δ_n)=(I_n:f_{J_0}), this contradicts linear independence modulo the colon ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3, Hilbert series of the superspace coinvariant ring
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For SR_n=Ω_n/SI_n, where q records polynomial x-degree and z records fermionic θ-degree, the bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(SR_n;q,z) = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k), where Stir_q(n,k) is defined by Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n−1,k)+Stir_q(n−1,k−1), Stir_q(0,0)=1, and Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each J⊆[n], choose a homogeneous basis B_n(J) of C[x]/(I_n:f_J). By Theorem 4.12 its degree generating function is ∏_i [st(J)_i]_q. Lemma 5.2 shows that ⨆_J B_n(J)θ_J is linearly independent in SR_n, so Hilb(SR_n;q,z) is at least ∑_J z^{|J|}∏_i[st(J)_i]_q. Proposition 3.7 gives the reverse inequality from the straightening spanning set. Thus equality holds, and Lemma 2.1 rewrites the subset-staircase sum as the q-Stirling expression.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.4, basis recipe from colon-ideal bases
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each J⊆[n], let B_n(J)⊆C[x_1,...,x_n] be a set of polynomials and let B_n=⨆_{J⊆[n]} B_n(J)θ_J⊆Ω_n. The following are equivalent: (1) for every J, B_n(J) descends to a basis of C[x]/(I_n:f_J); (2) the set ⨆_{J⊆[n]} { (b_J θ_J ⊙ D_J(δ_n)) ⊙ D_J(δ_n) : b_J∈B_n(J) } is a basis of the superharmonic space SH_n. Either condition implies that B_n descends to a basis of SR_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from (1) to a basis of SR_n is the proof of Theorem 5.3. For equivalence with the harmonic basis, define a linear map Ψ:⊕_J C[x]/(I_n:f_J)→SH_n by Ψ((h_J)_J)=∑_J (h_J θ_J⊙D_J(δ_n))⊙D_J(δ_n). This is well-defined because the θ_J coefficient of D_J(δ_n) is ±f_J⊙δ_n, and (I_n:f_J) annihilates f_J⊙δ_n. The domain and codomain have equal dimension by Theorems 4.12 and 5.3. Surjectivity follows by Gale induction: modulo lower-Gale fermionic terms, (C[x]θ_J⊙D_J(δ_n))⊙D_J(δ_n) recovers C[x]⊙D_J(δ_n), and Theorem 5.1 says these pieces generate SH_n. Therefore Ψ is an isomorphism, proving the equivalence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.6 and Proposition 5.7, terminal J-Artin bases
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For r≥1 and 1≤p≤n+1, let J_{r,p,n} be the ideal in C[x_1,...,x_n] generated by ∂_1 h_r,...,∂_{p−1}h_r,∂_p h_{r+1},...,∂_n h_{r+1}, where h_d means h_d(x_1,...,x_n). Then the monomials M_{r,p,n}={x_1^{b_1}...x_n^{b_n}: b_i
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 5.6, first show h_r∈J_{r,p,n} using the Euler-type identity x_1∂_1h_r+...+x_{p−1}∂_{p−1}h_r+∂_p h_{r+1}+...+∂_n h_{r+1}=C h_r with C>0. This also implies x_p J_{r,p+1,n}⊆J_{r,p,n}. One obtains an exact sequence C[x]/J_{r,p+1,n} --×x_p→ C[x]/J_{r,p,n} → C[¯ x]/¯ J_{r,p,n−1} → 0, where ¯ x deletes x_p. Regularity and Hilbert-series dimensions make the first map injective, giving a short exact sequence. Induction on n and p, together with the decomposition M_{r,p,n}=x_p M_{r,p+1,n} ⊔ ¯ M_{r,p,n−1}, proves the monomial basis. For Proposition 5.7, Theorem 4.12 gives explicit generators of (I_n:f_J): h_1(x_1,...,x_n),...,h_r(x_r,...,x_n) and the derivatives ∂_{r+1}h_{r+1},...,∂_n h_{r+1} in the terminal variables. The first generators triangularly reduce exponents in variables x_i for i≤r to a_i<i, and Lemma 5.6 handles the remaining derivative quotient, giving exactly the inequalities a_i<st(J)_i. The count agrees with Theorem 4.12, so spanning implies basis.

proof_techniques.name: One-boson/one-fermion superspace as a hook detector
proof_techniques.description: The paper studies Ω_n=C[x_1,...,x_n]⊗∧{θ_1,...,θ_n}, with one commuting and one anticommuting copy of the defining permutation representation. Its Hilbert series has two grading variables: q for bosonic degree and z for fermionic degree. The main theorem gives a completely explicit formula for this q,z series, and Lemma 2.1 refines it into a sum over subsets J with staircase products ∏[st(J)_i]_q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original universal Schur coefficient c_{(a,1^b)}(n) is related to the q^a z^b component of this superspace quotient by the usual diagonal supersymmetry/hook specialization, then this paper directly supplies the desired combinatorial interpretation: count subset-staircase exponent vectors, equivalently take the coefficient of q^a in [n-b]!_q Stir_q(n,n-b).

proof_techniques.name: Gale-order triangular straightening
proof_techniques.description: The paper orders fermionic monomials θ_J by Gale order and constructs, for each J and each i, elements q_{J,i}∈SI_n whose leading fermionic term is p_{J,i}θ_J and whose remaining terms are strictly higher in Gale order. Because the p_{J,i} form regular sequences, one can straighten any fθ_J modulo SI_n into a chosen basis modulo the p-ideal plus higher-Gale errors. Induction over Gale order then gives a global spanning set and a Hilbert-series upper bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a way to prove hook coefficient formulas without first finding an explicit monomial basis of the full multigraded diagonal coinvariant algebra. A solver can try to isolate hook components by a similar triangular filtration indexed by fermionic subsets.

proof_techniques.name: Regular sequences from complete-homogeneous derivative identities
proof_techniques.description: The regularity of p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} is not obtained by Gröbner bases. Instead, the paper proves a special identity among derivatives of complete homogeneous symmetric polynomials, uses it to force certain partial derivatives ∂_j h_{n-|J|+1}(J) into the p-ideal, then invokes a no-common-zero result for those derivatives and triangularity after setting J-variables to zero. Lemma 2.2 converts the resulting zero-locus statement into regularity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook shapes, the relevant enumerator is a product of q-integers from these regular sequences. If an attempted proof needs a finite-dimensional quotient with Hilbert series ∏[st(J)_i]_q, this provides the exact construction and regularity proof.

proof_techniques.name: Triangular differential operators D_J for lower bounds
proof_techniques.description: The paper builds differential operators D_J using minors of a lower-triangular matrix H of complete homogeneous polynomials. Applied to the Vandermonde δ_n, D_J(δ_n) has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n and only Gale-lower fermionic terms. This gives a triangular dual family to the q_{J,i} straightening elements and is the key to proving linear independence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When upper bounds come from straightening, these D_J operators give matching lower bounds. In a hook-coefficient proof, they can certify that all subset-staircase contributions are genuinely present rather than merely spanning candidates.

proof_techniques.name: Colon ideals (I_n:f_J) as the commutative shadow of fermionic degree J
proof_techniques.description: The leading coefficient f_J=∏_{j∈J}x_j∏_{i>j}(x_j-x_i) controls the θ_J piece of D_J(δ_n). The annihilator of f_J⊙δ_n is the colon ideal (I_n:f_J). Theorem 4.12 proves that this colon ideal equals the regular-sequence ideal generated by p_{J,i}, so its Hilbert series is exactly ∏[st(J)_i]_q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This identifies the algebraic object whose graded dimension supplies each subset-J contribution to a hook coefficient. It also gives a concrete route to constructing bases or statistics if the advisor wants more than a generating-function interpretation.

proof_techniques.name: Orbit harmonics and J-Nietzschean permutations
proof_techniques.description: The paper realizes (I_n:f_J) as gr I(N_J), where N_J is a subset of permutations defined by avoiding j-resentment conditions for all j∈J. This uses a deformation of the classical coinvariant ideal to the finite orbit of (1,2,...,n), removes the zero locus of an inhomogeneous polynomial ~f_J, and passes to top homogeneous parts. The resulting quotient has dimension |N_J|=∏ st(J)_i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a permutation-set model behind each staircase product. If the original problem asks for a more explicitly combinatorial interpretation of hook coefficients, one can combine J-Nietzschean permutations with a graded statistic coming from orbit harmonics or use the simpler exponent-vector model from the product formula.

proof_techniques.name: Poincare-duality comparison to prove colon equality
proof_techniques.description: After proving only the inclusion (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n})⊆(I_n:f_J), the paper upgrades it to equality using a Poincare-duality lemma. The ingredients are: C[x]/I_n has socle degree binomial(n,2), C[x]/(p_{J,i}) is a complete intersection with socle degree ∑st(J)_i−n, f_J has degree ∑(i−st(J)_i), and f_J∉I_n when 1∉J. The socle degrees add exactly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a compact way to prove that an apparently larger annihilator/colon ideal has no hidden generators. It may be useful whenever hook components are described as annihilators of differentiated Vandermondes.

proof_techniques.name: Sandwich proof of the Hilbert series
proof_techniques.description: The final Hilbert-series proof is a dimension sandwich. The straightening argument gives Hilb(SR_n)≤∑_J z^{|J|}∏[st(J)_i]_q. The D_J/colon-ideal argument gives a linearly independent set of exactly the same degree generating function, hence Hilb(SR_n)≥the same sum. Lemma 2.1 then rewrites the common expression as the q-Stirling formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This proof structure is robust: one can establish a hook formula by independently proving a spanning upper bound and a harmonic lower bound, even if no canonical full basis of the multigraded algebra is known.

proof_techniques.name: Basis assembly from smaller commutative quotients
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.4 says that any choice of bases B_n(J) for the quotients C[x]/(I_n:f_J) assembles into a basis ⨆_J B_n(J)θ_J of SR_n, and equivalently into a basis of the superharmonic space through the D_J operators. Thus the superspace quotient decomposes, at the level of bases and Hilbert series, into a family of ordinary polynomial quotients indexed by subsets J.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients, this means one can focus on interpreting or choosing bases for the colon quotients indexed by subsets of size b, rather than attacking the full m-variable diagonal coinvariant algebra at once.

other_useful_info: Combining Theorem 5.3 with Lemma 2.1 gives the explicit coefficient form inside the paper's setting: the coefficient of q^a z^b in Hilb(SR_n;q,z) is the number of pairs (J,α) such that J⊆[n], |J|=b, 0≤α_i<st(J)_i=i−|J∩[i]| for every i, and α_1+...+α_n=a; equivalently it is [q^a]([n-b]!_q Stir_q(n,n-b)). Subsets J containing 1 contribute zero because st(J)_1=0. At q=z=1 the Hilbert series gives ∑_{k=1}^n k! Stir(n,k), the ordered Bell number. The paper proves only the bigraded Hilbert series, not the full bigraded S_n-Frobenius characteristic; the Fields conjecture grFrob(SR_n;q,z)=∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} Δ′_{e_{k-1}} e_n |_{t=0} remains open in the paper. Conjecture 5.5 proposes a concrete monomial basis for each colon quotient: A_n(J)={x^a:a_i<st(J)_i} should be a basis of C[x]/(I_n:f_J); if true, ⨆_J A_n(J)θ_J is the Sagan-Swanson Artin-like basis of SR_n. Proposition 5.7 proves this monomial-basis conjecture for terminal Gale-maximal subsets J={r+1,...,n}. The paper points to Sagan-Swanson for the q-Stirling staircase identity and to Swanson-Wallach for prior harmonic bounds and the no-common-zero lemma used in the regular-sequence proof.
why_relevant: Computes the doubly graded Hilbert series of the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring and proves an operator theorem for its harmonics. Via diagonal supersymmetry, this is one of the most direct sources for extracting and interpreting hook coefficients c_(a,1^b)(n).
